ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese clay pot braised chicken rice; 'cooked rice dish' (FOODON_03315595) used as the closest parent class. A more specific term for Vietnamese clay pot rice dishes is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (rice-based dish with meat) is the closest defensible parent match. No specific code exists for Vietnamese clay pot braised chicken rice in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Vietnamese clay pot braised chicken rice. SNOMED CT does not catalogue specific ethnic rice dishes at this level of granularity.

serving.description: 1 set (1 portion clay pot braised chicken with 1 plain rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of braised lemongrass chicken (approximately 150 g) served with one bowl of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g), as typically served in Vietnamese casual dining in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Clay Pot Rice With Braised Chicken And Lemongrass (Cơm Gà Kho Xả) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Clay Pot Rice With Braised Chicken And Lemongrass (Cơm Gà Kho Xả), record ID G00010568. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient values not reported by HPB are set to -1 where not available, with the exception of potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol which are reported in the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — White rice (steamed/boiled) analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese clay pot braised chicken rice. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (jasmine-type), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The braised chicken and lemongrass components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 45.1 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 32.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100g; estimated 808 mg per 350 g serving, approaching 35% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce in the braising preparation and remove chicken skin to lower both sodium and saturated fat intake; the potassium content (327 mg/100g, ~1145 mg per serving) provides a meaningful but insufficient offset to the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) driven by white rice base; glycemic load per serving approximately 32.5, which is high and will produce a significant postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a serving, consider substituting brown rice to reduce GI to approximately 50–55, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response; the dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g and chicken protein provide modest mitigation but are insufficient to offset the high GL of a full serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and unknown saturated fat content from chicken skin and cooking oil; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g by HPB but this may reflect a measurement artefact for a chicken-containing dish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request skin-off chicken and minimal cooking oil to reduce saturated fat exposure, limit serving size to manage sodium intake, and note that the lemongrass and garlic aromatics provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols that may offer modest cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lemongrass citral compounds and garlic polyphenols provide anti-inflammatory benefit; chili (nightshade) may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of lemongrass and garlic; however, those sensitive to nightshades should request chili-free preparation and reduce saturated fat intake by removing chicken skin.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clay Pot Rice with Braised Chicken and Lemongrass (Vietnamese: Cơm Gà Kho Xả) is a Vietnamese rice dish consisting of steamed white jasmine rice served with chicken pieces braised in a fragrant sauce of lemongrass, fish sauce, garlic, black pepper, and chili. The dish is traditionally cooked in a clay pot, which imparts a subtle earthiness and retains heat, resulting in tender, deeply flavoured chicken with a slightly caramelised braising sauce. The flavour profile is savoury, aromatic, and mildly spicy, with the citral compounds in lemongrass providing a bright, citrus-herbal note that balances the umami depth of fish sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with notable dietary fibre at 3.7 g and potassium at 327 mg. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, reflecting the fish sauce content. The dish is served as a set meal in Vietnamese casual dining establishments in Singapore and Vietnam. Clinically, the high-GI white rice base (GI ~72) produces a significant glycaemic load per serving (~32.5), making portion control important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The sodium content warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The lemongrass and garlic aromatics contribute anti-inflammatory polyphenols relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record G00010568), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. The record is marked as reviewed with confidence 0.95 in the input. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate analogue, which is a well-established and defensible estimation basis.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this Vietnamese clay pot braised chicken rice dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010568) as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this dish. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB record G00010568 and no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match for this specific dish preparation. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; confirmed zero per HPB source G00010568; this may reflect rounding or measurement methodology for a chicken-containing dish. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white jasmine rice per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical Vietnamese restaurant preparation in Singapore; individual establishments should be verified. 6. Vietnamese dish — Vietnamese local name (Cơm Gà Kho Xả) is mandatory and present per G6 multilingual rule. Chinese names added as supplementary aliases given Singapore serving context. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable as this is a Vietnamese (not Singaporean) dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed g5_ontology_honesty failure by changing foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' and foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'. The validator requires match_type values to be one of ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; 'broader' is not an accepted value. Updated gap notes accordingly to reflect 'closest parent' terminology.
